facts_disclosed: Switched to Samsung tablet for news, print delivery ended

turns.content: Good evening. I find myself in an unfamiliar situation and would appreciate some guidance. My son recently gave me a Samsung tablet, and I am trying to use it to read the newspaper. Could you help me with some basics — adjusting text size, saving an article for later?

Good evening! I'd be happy to help you get comfortable with the tablet.

**Adjusting text size:**
- In most news apps, you can "pinch to zoom" — place two fingers on the screen and spread them apart to enlarge, or pinch together to shrink.
- For a permanent change, go to **Settings > Display > Font size and style** and drag the slider larger.

**Saving articles:**
- Most news apps have a bookmark icon — usually a small ribbon or flag shape. Tap it and the article saves for later reading.
- Some apps also have an "offline reading" option to download articles without needing internet.

Which newspaper app are you using? I can give more specific directions.

The Dong-A Ilbo app. They stopped delivering the physical paper in my area — not enough subscribers to justify the route, apparently. So Joon-seo, my son, bought the tablet when he visited from Seoul and set up the news apps for me. He was very patient, but now that he has gone home I find I have forgotten half of what he showed me.

That's very common — there's a lot to absorb all at once, and it's much easier to remember when someone is there guiding you. The fact that the delivery ended rather than this being your choice makes the transition harder too.

For the Dong-A Ilbo app specifically:

- **Main page**: You should see section tabs across the top — politics, economy, society, opinion, etc. Tap each one to browse that section, similar to flipping to that part of the physical paper.
- **Reading an article**: Tap the headline to open the full text. Pinch-to-zoom works within the article.
- **Going back**: The left-pointing arrow at the top of the screen, or the triangle button at the bottom, takes you to the previous page.
- **Bookmarking**: Look for a small ribbon icon at the top of an article. Tap it to save.

Is there a specific part that's giving you the most trouble?

The most frustrating aspect is not any single function. It is the feel of the thing. I have read a physical newspaper every morning for over fifty years. There is a ritual to it — the weight of the paper, the sound of the pages, the ink that sometimes marks your fingers. The tablet has none of this. The screen is cold and silent. I read faster on it, which should be an advantage, but I think I retain less. The experience is thinner somehow.

What you're describing is real and supported by research. Studies on reading comprehension have found that people often retain more from physical print than from screens, partly because the tactile and spatial experience of a physical page creates additional memory cues — where an article sat on the page, whether it was above or below the fold. These details anchor the content in memory.

The ritual aspect matters too. Fifty years of morning newspaper reading isn't just information retrieval; it's a sensory and emotional routine. The tablet delivers the same words but strips away the context that made the ritual meaningful.

That said, has anything about the tablet been useful, even reluctantly?

I will grant it one thing. I can enlarge the text, which my eyes appreciate greatly. Small print has become difficult for me, and the physical paper was getting harder to read comfortably. So in a purely practical sense, the tablet solves a problem I was trying to ignore. I think this is what they call progress — it solves one problem while creating a different kind of loss.

That's a very honest and precise way to frame it — gain in one dimension, loss in another, both simultaneously true.

The text enlargement is genuinely important, though. Comfortable reading without straining matters more than it might seem — eye strain affects concentration and even mood over time. If the tablet lets you read longer without discomfort, that's a real gain.

Since readability is the main benefit, let me suggest optimizing for it:

- Check if the app has a **dark mode** (white text on dark background). Many find this easier on the eyes, especially in evening reading.
- Set the system font to a comfortable default size so you don't have to zoom each time.
- Adjust screen brightness — moderate for morning reading, dimmer for evening.

Also, one other annoyance you may have noticed: notifications. If the tablet is making sounds and showing pop-up messages that interrupt your reading, swipe down from the top and look for "Do Not Disturb." Tap it, and everything goes silent while you read.

Yes, the notifications have been bothersome. I will try that setting. My mornings have mostly adjusted — I still wake at the same time, make barley tea, sit in the same chair. But instead of reaching for the paper, I reach for this glass rectangle. The tea is the same. The chair is the same. The light through the window is the same. But the center of the ritual has changed. Joon-seo said, 'Abeoji, in six months you will wonder how you managed without it.' I told him the probability of that outcome was quite low.

Your son may be more right than you'd like to admit — familiarity has a way of converting reluctant users. But even if you never love it, tolerating it comfortably while appreciating the larger text is a perfectly reasonable outcome.

The fact that you've preserved everything else about the morning ritual — the time, the tea, the chair, the light — suggests you're instinctively holding onto what you can while adapting to what you must. Over time, the tablet will find its own place in that architecture. It won't replace the newspaper in memory, but it can become its own familiar object.

Good evening. I hope tomorrow morning's reading is a little more peaceful with the notifications silenced.

Thank you. You have been very patient with what must seem like elementary questions. I spent decades surrounded by technology at the university but engaged with it only as much as duty required. Now duty has found me in retirement. But the guidance is appreciated. Good evening.

Nothing elementary about learning a new system — it's a genuine transition regardless of background. And approaching it with honesty about what's been lost alongside what's been gained is more thoughtful than most people manage. Good evening, and do ask your son about a tablet stand — it helps the reading feel less like holding a device and more like consulting a page.
